sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Diving emergencies include decompression sickness, arterial gas embolism, and pulmonary barotrauma. Prehospital priorities are high-concentration oxygen, careful history of the dive profile, supportive care, and early coordination with a hyperbaric-capable service.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Symptoms developing during or after a dive, sometimes within minutes of surfacing
- Joint pain, fatigue, rash, paraesthesia, weakness, or poor coordination
- Headache, confusion, visual or speech disturbance, seizure, or reduced conscious state
- Chest pain, cough, dyspnoea, cyanosis, or subcutaneous emphysema
- Collapse or dysrhythmia suggesting coronary or cerebral gas embolism

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Record dive depth, duration, repetitive dives, ascent details, surface interval, and time of symptom onset if known
- Definitive treatment for decompression illness is hyperbaric recompression and should be arranged early
- High-concentration oxygen is beneficial even when the final diagnosis is uncertain
- Position supine if tolerated, but allow position of comfort if this is needed for severe respiratory distress

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Administer high-concentration oxygen early unless there is a specific reason not to
- Keep the patient warm and monitor for hypothermia
- Perform frequent reassessment of neurological status, respiratory effort, and perfusion
- Minimise further exertion and prioritise urgent transport
- Pre-notify early if hyperbaric referral is likely

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Gain IV access where significant symptoms, shock, or neurological deficit are present
- Consider isotonic fluid therapy when dehydration or hypoperfusion is suspected, with repeated reassessment
- Provide analgesia within scope where needed
- Escalate early to retrieval, toxicology, or hyperbaric coordination pathways according to local system arrangements

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Arterial gas embolism often presents very soon after surfacing and can mimic stroke or seizure
- Joint pain within 24 to 48 hours after a dive should still raise concern for decompression sickness
- Document dive history, oxygen start time, neurological findings, and destination decisions clearly
